coredata.dc:title: Lysinoalanine formation and conformational characteristics of rice dreg protein isolates by multi-frequency countercurrent S-type sonochemical action
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yang
Zhang, Zhaoli
Li, Yihe
Jiang, Hui
Kumah Mintah, Benjamin
Dabbour, Mokhtar
He, Ronghai
Ma, Haile
coredata.dc:description: The influences of multi-frequency countercurrent S-type ultrasound (MFSU), with various frequency modes, on lysinoalanine (LAL) formation and conformational characteristics of rice dreg protein isolates (RDPI) were investigated. The ultrasonic operating mode with dual-frequency combination (20/40 kHz) indicated lower LAL content and higher protein dissolution rate of RDPI compared with that of other ultrasound operating modes. Under the dual-frequency ultrasound mode of 20/40 kHz, acoustic power density of 60 W/L, time of 20 min, and temperature of 35 °C, the relative reduction rate of LAL of RDPI reached the highest with its value of 26.95%, and the protein dissolution rate was 71.87%. The changes in chemical interactions between protein molecules indicated that hydrophobic interactions and disulfide bonds played a considerable role in the formation of LAL of RDPI, especially the reduction of g-g-g and g-g-t disulfide bond. Alterations in microstructure showed that ultrasonication loosened the protein structure and created more uniform protein fragments of RDPI. In conclusion, using MFSU in treating RDPI was an efficacious avenue for minimizing LAL content and modifying the conformational characteristics of RDPI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-frequency countercurrent S-type ultrasound (MFSU)
Rice dreg protein isolates (RDPI)
Lysinoalanine (LAL)
Conformational characteristics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Development of new multi-mode ultrasonic equipment
Determination of relative reduction rate of LAL
Effects of ultrasonic operating modes on relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate of RDPI
Effects of ultrasonic operating parameters on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
Effects of ultrasonic power density on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
Effects of sonication time on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
Effects of sonication temperature on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
Effects of various ultrasonic treatment parameters on chemical interactions

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of Multiple frequency countercurrent S-type ultrasound (MFSU) equipment 1. Sample outlet; 2. Hose; 3. Reaction cavity; 4. Sample inlet; 5. Ultrasonic transducer; 6. Ultrasonic generator; 7. PLC control panel; 8. Power switch; 9. Operating light; 10. Power light; 11. Thermostat controller.

Effects of various ultrasonic working mode on relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate of RDPI (I) Mono-frequency; (II) Dual-frequency; (III) Tri-frequency; (IV) four-frequency and five-frequency.
Effects of various ultrasound working modes on LAL content and protein dissolution rate of RDPI.
Effects of ultrasound treatment conditions on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI (A) ultrasound power density; (B) ultrasound time; (C) ultrasound temperature.

SDS-PAGE of RDPI under various ultrasound treatment conditions (A) Ultrasonic power density of 60 W/L (time of 15 min, temperature of 30 °C); (B) Treatment time of 20 min (power density of 60 W/L, temperature of 30 °C); (C) Temperature of 35 °C (power density of 60 W/L, time of 20 min).

Raman spectrum of RDPI under various ultrasound treatment conditions (A) Ultrasonic power density of 60 W/L (time of 15 min, temperature of 30 °C); (B) Treatment time of 20 min (power density of 60 W/L, temperature of 30 °C); (C) Temperature of 35 °C (power density of 60 W/L, time of 20 min).

Conformation change of disulfide bonds of RDPI under various ultrasound treatment conditions (A) Ultrasonic power density of 60 W/L (time of 15 min, temperature of 30 °C); (B) Treatment time of 20 min (power density of 60 W/L, temperature of 30 °C); (C) Temperature of 35 °C (power density of 60 W/L, time of 20 min).

The effects of various ultrasound treatment conditions on surface morphology (A) ultrasonic power density of 60 W/L (time of 15 min, temperature of 30 °C); (B) treatment time of 20 min (power density of 60 W/L, temperature of 30 °C); (C) temperature of 35 °C (power density of 60 W/L, time of 20 min).
The effects of various ultrasound conditions on the chemical interactions of RDPI.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Different letters were used to show significant differences (P < 0.05) in the same column.

head.ce:title: Lysinoalanine formation and conformational characteristics of rice dreg protein isolates by multi-frequency countercurrent S-type sonochemical action

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yang Wang and Zhaoli Zhang contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The influences of multi-frequency countercurrent S-type ultrasound (MFSU), with various frequency modes, on lysinoalanine (LAL) formation and conformational characteristics of rice dreg protein isolates (RDPI) were investigated. The ultrasonic operating mode with dual-frequency combination (20/40 kHz) indicated lower LAL content and higher protein dissolution rate of RDPI compared with that of other ultrasound operating modes. Under the dual-frequency ultrasound mode of 20/40 kHz, acoustic power density of 60 W/L, time of 20 min, and temperature of 35 °C, the relative reduction rate of LAL of RDPI reached the highest with its value of 26.95%, and the protein dissolution rate was 71.87%. The changes in chemical interactions between protein molecules indicated that hydrophobic interactions and disulfide bonds played a considerable role in the formation of LAL of RDPI, especially the reduction of g-g-g and g-g-t disulfide bond. Alterations in microstructure showed that ultrasonication loosened the protein structure and created more uniform protein fragments of RDPI. In conclusion, using MFSU in treating RDPI was an efficacious avenue for minimizing LAL content and modifying the conformational characteristics of RDPI.

ce:keyword.ce:text: sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis

ce:section.ce:para: Rice dreg (RD), as a co-product of starch and sugar-producing industry, contains 50–70% (w/w, d.b) protein. It is usually used as raw material to prepare rice dreg protein isolates (RDPI) using alkaline extraction and acid precipitation method for dietary and other uses [1]. Alkali procedures, however, may also cause side reactions. These include the rearrangement and cross-coupling between protein molecules, leading to the formation of cross-linked amino acids such as lysinoalanine (LAL), lanthionine, and histidinoalanine [2,3]. Some studies have shown that protein-rich foods may contain different levels of LAL [4–12].

LAL is a cross-linked amino acid that could cause several changes in the biological, chemical, and nutritional properties of alkali-treated proteins. The presence of LAL may induce the destruction of amino acid (AA) side chains and the racemization of AA, reducing the content of essential AA (such as lysine, cystine, threonine, etc.) in proteins [13]. Meanwhile, the formation of LAL interferes and disrupts the activity of pepsin to some degree, leading to a significant decrease in the in-vitro digestion and absorption rate of the protein [14] or net protein utilization (NPU) [15]. In addition, LAL has also been linked to nutritional and toxicological impact, as it could result in large numbers of kidney cells in some animals and consequently interfere with DNA synthesis, mitosis, and as well necrosis of tubular cells [16,17]. Therefore, strategies to minimize and adequately control the content of LAL in food products (especially protein foods) need to be developed.

In literature, reseachers have shown that addition of different chemical substances can inhibit the formation of LAL to some extent, e.g. mercapto compounds, organic acids, metal salts, sugars, etc.. Liu et al. [7] examined the effect of sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP) on LAL formation of whey protein. They found that the LAL content was reduced by 35.5% when the amount of STPP reached 0.6% (w/w). Luo et al. [8] investigated the effect of sulfhydryl compounds on the formation of LAL in preserved eggs, and indicated that the substance effectively decreased the LAL content in albumen and yolk. Chang et al. [18] showed that the addition of 10 mM L-Cys or reduced glutathione (10 mM) solution to the marinade, resulted in the reduction of LAL content in preserved duck eggs. Finley et al. [19] showed that addition of mercapto AA to casein or soy protein during alkaline treatment significantly reduced LAL content. Friedman et al. [1] showed that nucleophiles could cleave protein disulfide bonds to generate free sulfur-containing cysteine residues, which might preferentially react with dehydroalaine (DHA) residues, suggesting nucleophiles could inhibit the formation of LAL. These studies have demonstrated that LAL formation in foods was suppressed to some extent by addition of chemical substances. Such method, however, might alter the original flavor and texture characteristics, and also reduce the nutritional value of the foods, e.g., deficiency of some amino acids [20]. Therefore, the current study focused on finding an alternative strategy to inhibit LAL formation or reduce its levels to preserve the nutritional and functional characteristics of proteins in food(s).

As a new non-thermal physical processing technology, ultrasonic technology has been widely used in the food industry [21–25]. Ultrasound treatment of various proteins has demonstrated significant effects on the protein structure, especially in the transformation of disulfide bonds to sulfhydryl groups [26–28]. Besides, some studies [1] have shown that sulfhydryl groups of sulfhydryl compounds could preferentially react with dehydroalanine (DHA) and consequently prevent the formation of LAL. So it is thought that ultrasonic technology could be applied to alkaline extraction of RDPI to reduce or control LAL formation. The ultrasonic working mode is a significant parameter that influences cavitation yield in solutions.

Compared with traditional single-frequency ultrasound, multi-frequency sonication produces more cavitation yield [29]. Bubbles formation can be improved up to 5-times more than in the single-frequency [30]. The apparatus of the multi-frequency countercurrent S-type ultrasound (MFSU) was used in this study, which was developed by our research team. It could treat samples by S-type circulation through cavity with particular geometry. This particular structure produces resonance under the combination of various frequency modes, so it is easier to produce ultrasonic cavitation effect by varying the with operating modes. Besides, ultrasonic treatment parameter is another important factor for the extraction of proteins [31]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no systematic research has been conducted using a sensitive ultrasound equipment like the MFSU in ultrasound-assisted extraction of raw materials. Moreover, there is no report on the effect of ultrasonic treatment on the formation of LAL and conformational characteristics of RDPI.

Therefore, the objectives of this study were to investigate the effects of MFSU operating modes and parameters on the LAL formation and structural characteristics of RDPI. We firstly studied the relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI under various ultrasonic operating modes (30 kinds of modes). Under the conditions of optimal ultrasonic operating modes, the effects of sonication parameters (power density, time, temperature) on the relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate of RDPI were investigated. In addition, the mechanism of ultrasonic treatment on LAL formation of RDPI was examined by studying the changes in protein structure. This work provides a fundamental information for inhibiting the formation of LAL by ultrasound action, and have practical guiding implications for nutrition and food safety in alkali-treatment food(s).

Rice dreg (75.51% protein-Kjeldahl Method) was obtained from Anhui Jintai Sugar Company (China). The classical extraction method with alkali (NaOH) concentration 0.09 M was utilised in extracting RDPI (35 °C, 120 min) followed by precipitation with an acidic solution [6]. The protein content of the sample was 90.2% (w/w) (Kjeldahl Method). All reagents used in this study were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of new multi-mode ultrasonic equipment
ce:section.ce:para: The MFSU equipment (Fig. 1 ) is equipped with generators (developed by our research group, Jiangsu University, China) at various frequencies, including 20, 28, 35, 40 and 52 kHz. The ultrasonic working modes can work in mono-frequency ultrasound (MFU), dual-frequency ultrasound (DFU), tri-frequency ultrasound (TFU), four-frequency ultrasound, and five-frequency ultrasound (FFU), including 30 kinds of working modes. The entire ultrasonic device is a divergent countercurrent system, and the materials can be treated by S-type circulation through cavity with particular geometry. Because of the diversity of the bottom geometry of the cavity, it can produce resonance under the combination of various frequency modes, so it is easier to produce ultrasonic cavitation effect.

The extraction of RDPI was done as outlined in our earlier work [20] with slight modifications. A 100 g of rice dreg was weighed, and the suspension (1:10 w/v) was prepared with alkaline (0.09 M NaOH) and stirred (using impeller agitator, 100 rpm) at 35 °C (120 min). The RDPI solution is circularly transported to the reaction vessel using peristaltic pump, and treated by sonication. The ultrasonic treatment conditions were as follows: ultrasonic power density of 40 W/L, time of 15 min, and temperature of 30 °C.

Ultrasonic power density, treatment time, and temperature were examined under the optimum frequency mode. The experiment was conducted under the following conditions: power density of 20–120 W/L; treatment time of 0–25 min; temperature of 25-50 °C. The relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI were set as indices for optimizing sonication operating modes and ultrasonic parameters. Control (RD samples) was prepared with the same conditions but without ultrasonication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of relative reduction rate of LAL
ce:section.ce:para: The LAL content of sonicated RDPI was determined following method of Zhang et al. [20] by High-Performance Liquid Chromatography.

The relative reduction rate of LAL was calculated as follows:
(1) Relative reduction rate of LAL (%) = [(L0 − Lu)/L0] × 100
where L0 is the LAL content of 0.09 M NaOH-treated samples (g/kg protein); Lu represents the LAL content of sonicated samples (g/kg protein).

The different sonicated RDPI solutions were centrifuged (10,000 g, 15 min, 4 °C), and the supernatants were collected. The content of protein eluted in the supernatant was determined according to the Folin-phenol method [32], which was denoted as C.
(2)
y =
C · V
m
× 100
where y (%) represents the protein dissolution rate; C (mg/L) represents soluble protein concentration; m (mg) is the initial mass of the ultrasonic treated RDPI; V (L) is the volume of the ultrasonically treated RDPI solution.
The chemical interactions of RDPI were examined as outlined by Cao et al. [33] with some modifications using 0.10 g sample (supernatant) following centrifugation (20,000 g, 20 min).

SDS-PAGE was conducted as described by Jiang et al. [34] with little modification. SDS-PAGE system was composed of 5% stacking and 12% resolving gel. The samples were mixed with the loading buffer (4:1 v/v), then the resultant solutions were boiled at 100 °C for 10 min and cooled immediately. 20 μL of the resultants were injected in stacking gel and performed at stacking voltage of 80 mV and the separation voltage of 120 mV.

Raman spectra were measured at room temperature (25 °C) by a laser confocal micro-Raman spectrometer coupled with a 785 nm near-infrared laser. Each spectrum was obtained under the following conditions: 300 mW of laser power, 4 scans, 60 s exposure time, wavenumber range 400–2000 cm−1. Spectral data processing, including smoothing, baseline correction and normalization to the intensity of phenylalanine peak at 1003 ± 1 cm−1, was performed using OMNIC (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Madison, WI, USA) and Perkin Elmer Spectrum version 10.00.00 software [35]. Raman Spectral Analysis Package Version 2.1 software was used to calculate disulfide bonds (SS) content changes of protein in various configurations.

The microstructure and surface roughness was recorded by Multimode microscope (Bruker, Santa Barbara, CA). Samples in PeakForce QNM mode using Bruker Scan Asyst needle were used for AFM observations (300 kHz, 25.1 N/m).

All data were presented as the means with a standard deviation of three determinations. Difference between means was compared using Tukey’s test (p < 0.05). Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS base 24.0 (IBM Corporation, NY, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ultrasonic operating modes on relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate of RDPI
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (I-IV) shows the influence of sonication operating modes on the relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate of RDPI. The results showed that the relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate of sonicated RDPI significantly increased (P < 0.05) compared with the control.

Fig. 2(I) showed the influence of MFU pretreatment on the relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI. From the results, it can be seen that when the ultrasonic frequency was 52 kHz, the relative reduction rate of LAL of RDPI reached the highest value (16.23%), and the protein dissolution rate was 62.72%. However, protein dissolution rate reached a maximum value of 70.70% at the frequency of 35 kHz, and the relative reduction rate of LAL was 14.96%. Thus, comprehensive consideration of these indicators showed that frequency with 35 kHz was more suitable to the protein dissolution, and also sharply reduced the LAL content of RDPI.

Fig. 2 (II) displayed the influence of DFU on the relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI. The results demonstrated that the relative reduction rate of LAL reached the highest value (24.37%) at 20/40 kHz. Moreover, the protein dissolution rate was 71.47%. The results were better than single frequency 20, 40 kHz working alone, and had a significant difference with other dual-frequency working modes (P > 0.05). The reason may be that different ultrasonic frequency modes may affect the cavitation effect.

There is interaction between different ultrasound waves under the action of multi-frequency ultrasound [36]. Thus, the dual-frequency (20/40 kHz) has a synergistic effect on the processing of raw protein. So, the DFU provides a wider spectrum range of action, which has a significant enhancement effect on the frequency of ultrasonic chemical reactions. As a result, the combined effect of DFU radiation is greater than the sum of the effects of MFU radiation alone [37,38]. However, the effect of 40/52 kHz DFU was less desirable compared to the single frequency; so it was not that the more the combined frequency was the better. Studies have shown that when the power density is constant, the liquid cavitation effect decreases with the increased frequency [39], and the faster the energy attenuation [40], the more the effects of substrate treatment.

As seen from Fig. 2(III), TFU treatment was applied to the alkaline extraction of RDPI. When the ultrasonic working mode was 20/28/35 kHz, the relative reduction rate of LAL reached the maximum (19.42%), reduced by 4.95% compared to that of 20/40 kHz. In addition, the protein dissolution rate was 62.48%. Similar observation was reported by Yang et al. [41]. Also, the outcome (Fig. 2IV) indicated that FFU influenced the LAL content and dissolution rate of RDPI. After FFU treatment, the relative reduction rate of LAL reduced to<12%, and the protein dissolution rate was relatively lower. Noteworthy, the relative reduction rate of LAL was significantly lower than that of MFU, DFU and TFU treatment. The observed phenomenon may be linked to gradually increasing combination frequency. Under these conditions, the absolute sound pressure of protein solution decreased, and the sound field distribution tends to be more uniform. The cavitation threshold, however, significantly became greater, making the intensity of cavitation effect more difficult [42]. Therefore, 20/40 kHz decreased the LAL content most compared to other modes, which showed that this mode was the most sensitive ultrasound frequency.

The relationship between the ultrasonic working mode, LAL content of RDPI and protein dissolution rate is displayed in Fig. 3 . The size of bubble represents the effect of ultrasonic working mode on the other two variables. The larger the bubble was, the more significant the influence was [43]. From the bubble chart, we observed that DFU was superior to MFU and other frequency modes (Fig. 3). Moreover, at 20/40 kHz DFU, the LAL content of RDPI was 10532.24 g/kgprotein, and decreased by 24.37% compared with non-sonicated sample, and the protein dissolution rate reached 71.47%. The decrease in LAL content with reference to respective ultrasonicated samples, particularly at 20/40 kHz treated samples, may be attributed to the conformation of the protein-converting disulfide bonds to sulfhydryl groups of RDPI, as a result of the cavitation forces by ultrasound action. Further, sonication could have caused significant increase in the sulfhydryl content of RDPI, which may have facilitated rapid reaction with DHA [44], and consequently reducing the possibility of a reaction between ε-NH2 of lysine and DHA to generate LAL was reduced. Therefore, ultrasonic treatment could inhibit the formation of LAL in RDPI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ultrasonic operating parameters on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
Effects of ultrasonic power density on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different ultrasonic power density on relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate were studied under the conditions of constant ultrasonic parameters (15 min, 30 °C) (Fig. 4 (A)). The relative reduction rate of LAL, firstly, increased and then decreased with increasing ultrasonic power density (Fig. 4(A)). When the ultrasonic power density was 60 W/L, the relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate reached a maximum value of 24.44% and 71.01%, respectively. This is because as the ultrasonic power density increases, the ultrasonic cavitation intensity also increases, resulting in the formation of high impact force on the protein surface. In addition, ultrasonication might have significantly increased the probability of the conversion of disulfide bonds into sulfhydryl groups in protein molecules, resulting in the change of the conformation of protein macromolecule [45]. So, ultrasonication has a significant effect on the proteins structure [46]. Therefore, the sulfhydryl groups could preferentially react with the double bond (C = C) of DHA to reduce the likelihood of a reaction between ε-NH2 of lysine and DHA to produce LAL; thus the LAL content in the protein decreased. In addition, the collapse of cavitation bubbles generated by ultrasound, may have caused rapid decreases in particle size of the protein, thereby increasing the dissolution of soluble protein. Under high ultrasonic power density (>60 W/L), relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate showed a decreasing trend. The possible reason was that with the continuous increasing of power density, ultrasonic intensity increased, resulting in excessive cavitation bubbles and the formation of sound barrier [47]. Thus, the cavitation intensity or cavitation effect was weakened, leading to the reduction of damage degree in protein structure. Besides, The aggregation of soluble proteins was not conducive to the improvement of protein dissolution. Therefore, the ultrasonic power density was selected as 60 W/L for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of sonication time on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different ultrasonic treatment time on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI at power density of 60 W/L and temperature of 30 °Cwere studied. The results are displayed in Fig. 4(B). With increasing time, the relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate increased and reached to the highest values at 20 min (24.40% and 70.89%, respectively). Moreover, the relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate decreased slightly when the time was more than 20 min. The reason for this phenomenon was that, as the processing time is extended, the more cavitation bubbles were generated in the appropriate ultrasonic time. The stronger the local shock wave generated after these bubbles were closed, resulting in the transition from disulfide bond to sulfhydryl group of intramolecular. However, with the prolongation of sonication time, the sulfhydryl groups are shifted to high-energy state due to the effect of continuous waves, resulting in the reduction of the activation energy from sulfhydryl groups to disulfide bonds [48]. Thus, some sulfhydryl groups were converted to disulfide bonds again, and relative reduction rate of LAL was reduced. Meanwhile, the most appropriate sonication time was suitable for sufficient swelling of the protein molecules, and the dissolution rate continued to increase. However, with the extension of pretreatment time, the protein solution system reached a dynamic equilibrium, and as a result the protein components in the system could not dissolve. Consequently, the protein dissolution rate was relatively stable. In the case of little decrease in protein dissolution rate caused by long ultrasonic treatment time, some studies showed that hydrophobic groups were exposed by ultrasound waves, leading to an increase in hydrophobicity, which then cause protein aggregation and folding [49,50]. Therefore, the optimal ultrasonic treatment time was selected as 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of sonication temperature on relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different temperature on the relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate of RDPI were examined under the conditions of 60 W/L and 20 min and the results are exhibited in Fig. 4(C). The relative reduction rate of LAL and protein dissolution rate increased with increasing sonication temperature, and reached the highest value at 35 °C (26.95% and 71.87%, respectively), then decreased gradually. The observation may be explained by the concept that as sonication temperature increases, the viscosity coefficient and surface tension of the medium also decreases, causing a reduction in cavitation threshold. So, it is likely to produce cavitation effect, strong shock wave, and micro-jet, which makes the surface structure of protein particles loose [51]. These phenomena are conducive for the generation of sulfhydryl groups. Moreover, the cleavage of disulfide bonds also leads to more stretching of the protein, resulting in exposure of the internal sulfhydryl groups [52,53]. Meanwhile, the Brownian movement of protein molecules was accelerated significantly, and the dissolution rate of soluble proteins increased [54]. However, when cavitation bubbles collapse at a higher temperature, the vapor pressure of the solution increased to accompany the maximum pressure value generated, inducing the reduction in cavitation action [51]. The results were consistent with those of Jin et al. [50] and Zhang et al. [55]. Therefore, the optimal ultrasonic temperature was selected as 35 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of various ultrasonic treatment parameters on chemical interactions
ce:section.ce:para: In the RDPI system, the interactions between protein molecules are mainly through ionic bonds, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and covalent bonds that maintained the tertiary structure [56]. As seen from Table 1 , ultrasonication might have altered the chemical interactions through the changes of the ionic bonds, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and disulfide bonds compared to the non-sonicated samples. With the increase of power density and processing time, the relative content of ionic bonds first increased and then decreased. When the power density was 60 W/L and the processing time was 20 min, the relative content of ionic bonds reached a maximum value. Meanwhile, the relative content of hydrophobic interaction exhibited the same trend with the relative content of ionic bond. Nevertheless, as sonication temperature increased, the relative content of ionic bond showed a decreasing trend, and the relative content of the hydrophobic interaction gradually increased. With the increase of ultrasonic power density, time, and temperature, the relative content of hydrogen bonds decreased. In addition, the relative content of disulfide bonds firstly decreased and then increased, but it was smaller than non-sonicated samples, especially the relative content of disulfide bonds appeared the minimum value under the ultrasonic conditions (60 W/L, 20 min, 35 °C).

Generally, ionic bonds, hydrogen bonds, and hydrophobic forces are non-covalent bond forces in protein molecules [56]. Disulfide bond is an important covalent bond to maintain the stability of protein spatial structure. Ionic and hydrogen bonds are weaker than hydrophobic interactions and disulfide bonds, which are easy to be destroyed [33]. During the process of alkaline extraction of RDPI, the number of negative charges in the protein was more than the number of positive charges. This part of negative charges was mainly derived from dissociation of ammonia ion (–NH3+) of acidic amino acid to form NH2RCHCOO-, resulting in the stretching of the protein and exposure of the internal sulfhydryl groups. However, sonication treatment could destroy the spatial structure of protein, leading to further entanglement of protein structure to some extent, and absolute exposure of polar groups such as charged group, hydrophobic group, and hydroxyl group. Meanwhile, the relative content of ion bond and hydrophobic interaction between proteins molecules also increased after sonication. Our findings are consistent with what is reported by other scholars [33] for other samples. Hydrogen bond was the main force to maintain the secondary structure of protein. The decrease of hydrogen bond content indicated that intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bond was broken, and the secondary structure was destroyed under the combined effect of mechanical and cavitation force. Huang et al. [57] found that the secondary structure of protein was connected by various types of hydrogen bond, and ultrasound could destroy some hydrogen bond structures. Disulfide bond was considered to be the main covalent bond that influences the formation of LAL. The protein structure was acted by ultrasound cavitation effect, and the disulfide bond between polypeptide chains would be converted to sulfhydryl group, which also indicated that the reduction of disulfide bond content represented increase in mercapto group. Some researchers showed that the mercapto group of a protein preferentially reacted with DHA, the intermediate product of LAL, and effectively inhibit the formation of LAL under the alkaline conditions. Therefore, the chemical interactions between protein molecules were altered significantly, among which the relative content of hydrophobic interaction and disulfide bond were the main factors affecting the LAL formation of RDPI.

The changes in RDPI subunits including aggregation generated by disulfide bonds and dissociation of each subunit under various sonication treatments can be analyzed by SDS-PAGE profile (Fig. 5 ). The migration speed of a protein molecule depends only on the molecular weight of the protein subunit. The protein showed typical acidic glutelin subunits-AS (30–39 KDa) and basic glutelin BS subunits (19–25 KDa). Compared with control, sonication did not induce significant changes in the subunits of protein. These results were consistent with the previous results. There was no dimer formation, and ultrasound mainly caused physical changes at the macromolecule level. Similar finding was alsoreported for soybean protein isolate [35]. Therefore, although protein molecules or intermolecular cross-linking might occur, and the protein bands of samples with various LAL content would not be changed under the alkaline condition by sonication. The possible reason was that the change in molecular protein weight of the protein caused by the difference in LAL content could not be reflected in the electrophoretic spectrum.

Fig. 6 showed typical spectra of RDPI by various ultrasonic treatments in the wavenumber of 400–1500 cm−1. The intensity and position of the disulfide bonds at the range of 500–550 cm−1 were sensitive to protein conformation after sonication. It reacts with different Raman shifts in different vibration modes. For example, at 500–510 cm−1 is gauche gauche gauche mode, 515–525 cm−1 is gauche gauche trans mode, 535–545 cm−1 is trans gauche trans mode [58]. The peak analyzer software was used for multi-peak guassina fitting to obtain the change in the relative content of disulfide bond configuration of RDPI, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 .

The disulfide bond (Fig. 7) of non-sonicated samples mainly existed in the g-g-g configuration, which was consistent with the results of przulj et al. [59] for soybean protein isolate. After ultrasonic treatment, the relative content of g-g-g and t-g-t configuration in the disulfide bonds decreased, and the relative content of g-g-t configuration increased, which may be due to the transformation of g-g-g and t-g-t configuration into g-g-t configuration. Therefore, the g-g-g and g-g-t disulfide bonds were likely to be altered and transformed into mercapto groups after ultrasonication, thus participating in the inhibition reaction of LAL formation in RDPI.

AFM could truly reflect the microscopic morphology of the protein at the nanometer level. The atomic force microscope images of RDPI following MFSU treatment are shown in Fig. 8 . After ultrasound treatment, the microstructure of RDPI had many micropores, and the granular structure became loose. Moreover, the sonicated sample displayed more disordered structures, with microparticles (Fig. 8). When ultrasonic power density was 60 W/L, the surface of the RDPI was filled with damaged micropores (Fig. 8b). Fig. 8(c,d) showed that the protein particles decreased in size, and then sharply increased number under different sonication conditions. This was in similitude with the findings of Zhang et al. [55]. The changes in the microstructure may be due to the cavitation bubbles generated by ultrasound which may have disrupted the aggregation between the native protein molecules. The results were consistent with the conclusions 3.3 and 3.4 in this experiment. Aslo, sonication time, had significant effect on the micro-morphology of RDPI as shown in Fig. 8 (b) and (c). With the increased time, the number of small protein particles also increased significantly, and the particle distribution was relatively uniform. Further, the height and size of protein particles increased to some extent (Fig. 8 (d)) with increases in ultrasonic/treatment temperature.

The difference in surface micro-morphology of RDPI would directly affect the formation of LAL [20]. This is mainly due to the redistribution of amino and carboxyl end of hydrophobic surface protein groups caused by the change of surface morphology, which affects the content of ionized lysine residue. In addition, the changes in protein surface morphology affected the cross-linking reaction between amino acid residues in protein, thereby influencing the formation rate of LAL.

All the ultrasonic operating modes significantly enhanced the relative reduction rate of LAL and the dissolution rate of RDPI. Dual-frequency mode of 20/40 kHz (at 60 W/L, 20 min, 35 °C) indicated the highest relative reduction rate of LAL and dissolution rate of RDPI. MFSU pretreatment resulted in the changes in chemical interactions between protein molecules; and hydrophobic interaction and disulfide bonds were the main factors influencing the LAL formation of RDPI. Moreover, g-g-g and g-g-t disulfide bonds might be involved in the inhibition of LAL formation. In addition, AFM revealed that ultrasound changed the microstructure of RDPI. Overall, ours observations demonstrated that ultrasonic operating modes had significant influence on the generation of LAL during the alkaline extraction of RDPI.

Yang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Data curation. Zhaoli Zhang: Software, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Yihe Li: Visualization, Supervision. Hui Jiang: Validation, Formal analysis. Benjamin Kumah Mintah: Writing - review & editing. Mokhtar Dabbour: Writing - review & editing. Ronghai He: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Resources. Haile Ma: Visualization, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by grants from Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (31972208), Jiangsu Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Fund (CX (18) 3038), and Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2018311), sponsored by a project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2020.105257.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemistry, biochemistry, nutrition, and microbiology of lysinoalanine, lanthionine, and histidinoalanine in food and other proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Friedman, Chemistry, Biochemistry, Nutrition, and Microbiology of Lysinoalanine, Lanthionine, and Histidinoalanine in Food and Other Proteins, Journal of aAgricultural and fFood cChemistry 47 (1999) 1295–1319.

Ma, Alkali solution extraction of rice residue protein isolates: Influence of alkali concentration on protein functional, structural properties and lysinoalanine formation, Food Chemistry 218 (2017) 207–215.
